name: Radical Atoms
short_description: A vision for transformable physical materials that are as dynamically reconfigurable as on-screen pixels and bidirectionally coupled to an underlying digital model.
long_description: Radical Atoms, proposed by Hiroshi Ishii and colleagues at the MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group, extends the earlier Tangible Bits agenda toward a future in which the material itself becomes the interface. The vision imagines computationally transformable matter that changes its shape, appearance, and properties in real time, kept in two-way sync with a digital model so physical and digital states continuously reflect one another. As physical AI and interaction design, it reframes embodiment itself as a malleable medium rather than a fixed enclosure for computation, anticipating shape-changing and programmable-matter interfaces. It is an articulated vision and research agenda rather than a single built system.
ai_note: Radical Atoms is a vision about computationally transformable, bidirectionally coupled materials and tangible interaction; it does not posit or implement AI/ML.

images.caption: Diagram of the Tangible Bits to Radical Atoms progression and the Material User Interface concept from the Tangible Media Group vision page.
images.alt_text: A conceptual diagram illustrating the evolution from Tangible Bits to Radical Atoms, showing materials whose physical form is bidirectionally coupled with an underlying digital model.

sources.title: Radical Atoms (Vision)
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab, Tangible Media Group

sources.title: Radical atoms: beyond tangible bits, toward transformable materials

interactions.title: Sculpt clay that snaps to perfect geometry
interactions.description: In the Perfect Red scenario, you roll a lump of the material in your hands and it snaps into a true sphere, then join two pieces and the shapes Boolean-add together — physical sculpting that carries CAD logic inside the matter itself.
interactions.source_label: MIT Tangible Media Group — Perfect Red

interactions.title: Cut and pin like a parametric tool
interactions.description: Draw a line along the surface and tap it with a knife to cut along that spline, or draw ten dots and stick a pin into one to drill all ten holes at once — hand gestures that trigger parametric, repeatable operations in the live material.
interactions.source_label: MIT Tangible Media Group — Perfect Red

interactions.title: Split a piece and edit both halves at once
interactions.description: Divide the material into two linked parts so that an edit you make to one half is instantly mirrored in the other, keeping the physical object in two-way sync with its underlying computational model.
interactions.source_label: MIT Tangible Media Group — Perfect Red

interactions.title: The material tells you what it can become
interactions.description: Radical Atoms are framed by three behaviors — transform (change shape to reflect the model), conform (yield to constraints from the environment and your hands), and inform (signal their own dynamic affordances) — so the surface itself shows you the moves it will allow rather than relying on a screen or manual.

interactions.title: Push a pin-array surface up into a 3D model
interactions.description: inFORM, the group's working shape display built toward this vision, renders 3D content as a grid of motorized pins you can touch and reshape, and pushes back to move real objects sitting on the table — a buildable step toward matter that mirrors a digital model.

interactions.title: Shake hands with a remote collaborator's shape
interactions.description: Over a video link, inFORM raises its surface to physically reproduce a distant participant's hands, so a remote person can nudge an object or 'reach across' the table and you feel the shape they make.

interactions.title: Walk through the vision at the MIT Museum
interactions.description: The MIT Museum has staged a Radical Atoms exhibition where visitors encounter the Tangible Media Group's transformable-interface work in person, bringing the lab vision out to a public audience.

interactions.title: Knead a budget into a hillside
interactions.description: Press your thumbs into a tabletop of the material to carve a terrain model, and have it conform to a real watershed constraint — every dent reshaping the underlying simulation and snapping back when a rule is violated, grounded in the transform/conform coupling the vision specifies.

interactions.title: Pull a part back to an earlier shape
interactions.description: Because each physical edit is bound to a computational model, you could scrub the object's history — flatten a finished bracket back to its blank and replay the changes — treating undo and version control as something you do with your hands on the material.
